lessons.title: Introduction & Overview
lessons.content: {"simpleExplanation":"CALCULATE is one of the most powerful and commonly used functions in DAX. It modifies the filter context of a calculation and then evaluates the expression within that new context. Syntax: CALCULATE(<expression>, <filter1>, <filter2>, ...) How it Works: The first argument is an expression (like SUM(Sales[Amount])).","whyItMatters":["Provides a robust foundation for advanced topics.","Improves system design and code maintainability.","Frequently asked in technical interviews."],"example":"// Example Concept: Introduction & Overview\n// Apply these principles in your projects to ensure robust functionality.","summary":"FILTER() Purpose: FILTER returns a table that includes only the rows that meet a specified condition. It's often used inside CALCULATE to apply more complex filter logic.","practiceTask":"Review Introduction & Overview and identify 3 real-world applications where this principle is used."}

lessons.title: FILTER(<table>, <condition>) Example:
lessons.content: {"simpleExplanation":"High Sales = CALCULATE(SUM(Sales[Amount]), FILTER(Sales, Sales[Amount] > 1000)) This calculates the total sales where each sale is greater than 1000. Information Technology Services Key Points: FILTER does not perform the calculation—it only returns a filtered table. It allows row-by-row evaluation of conditions, unlike simple filter expressions in CALCULATE.","whyItMatters":["Provides a robust foundation for advanced topics.","Improves system design and code maintainability.","Frequently asked in technical interviews."],"example":"// Example Concept: FILTER(<table>, <condition>) Example:\n// Apply these principles in your projects to ensure robust functionality.","summary":"ALL() Purpose: ALL removes filters from a column or table, returning all rows, regardless of the current filter context. It's useful for calculating totals, ignoring slicers, or creating percentages and rankings.","practiceTask":"Review FILTER(<table>, <condition>) Example: and identify 3 real-world applications where this principle is used."}

lessons.content: {"simpleExplanation":"Role-Playing Dimensions in Power BI (e.g., Multiple Date Fields) In Power BI, a role-playing dimension refers to a single dimension table (like a Date or Customer table) that can be used in multiple roles within a data model. This scenario is common when a fact table (like Sales or Orders) contains multiple foreign keys that relate back to the same dimension table but for different purposes. A typical example is a Date dimension that plays different roles such as Order Date, Ship Date, Delivery Date, etc.","whyItMatters":["Provides a robust foundation for advanced topics.","Improves system design and code maintainability.","Frequently asked in technical interviews."],"example":"// Example Concept: Information Technology Services\n// Apply these principles in your projects to ensure robust functionality.","summary":"So, if your Sales table contains both OrderDate and ShipDate, and both relate to the same Date table, only one relationship can be active at once. lessons.content: {"simpleExplanation":"You can use the selected parameter value in DAX formulas to dynamically update measures.
